one_line_takeaway: UCAN gives a universal, theoretically-sound recipe (with a closed soundness gap versus prior input-dependent methods) for turning any isotropic randomized-smoothing certified defense into an anisotropic one, yielding large certified-accuracy gains at large certified radii (up to 182.6% on CIFAR10, 142.5% on MNIST, 121.1% on ImageNet) at the cost of only supporting independent (diagonal-covariance) per-dimension noise in practice.
key_finding: Against l2 perturbations with Gaussian noise, UCAN's certification-wise variant improves certified accuracy over the best prior SOTA baseline by up to 142.5% at large radius on MNIST (e.g., ALM-based certified accuracy 94% vs. baseline decaying to near 0% by radius 2.25; the paper's headline abstract number is 182.6%, which corresponds to CIFAR10 at radius 2.25 where UCAN's radius-based certified accuracy reaches 65% vs. the best baseline's much lower value), and 121.1% on ImageNet at the largest evaluated radius. Concretely, on CIFAR10 (Table IV) UCAN's certified-accuracy-w.r.t.-radius improves over the best baseline by +182.6% at radius 2.25 and by +151.9% at radius 2.00, while remaining competitive or best at small radii too (0% to +5.2% improvement at radius <=0.5). On MNIST (Table III) the improvement grows from -1.0% at radius 0 (i.e., UCAN is not always best at the smallest radius) up to +135.0%/+142.5% (ALM/radius) at radius 2.25. Beyond l2, on CIFAR10 with l1 perturbations, anisotropic Laplace noise reaches 61% certified accuracy at radius 2.0 versus <=25% for the l1 baselines cited (Teng et al., Yang et al.); with l_inf perturbations, anisotropic Gaussian reaches 73% at 6/255 versus 31-38% for Cohen et al. and Zhang et al. baselines. Ablation across the three NPGs shows certification-wise (input-specific mean+variance) dominates dataset-wise and pattern-wise across settings; dataset-wise gives large ALM gains (up to ~6x on MNIST) but only modest radius gains, since it can shrink variance on important dimensions while inflating it elsewhere to preserve the mean.
core_contribution: The paper proposes UCAN (Universally Certifies adversarial robustness with Anisotropic Noise), a technique that upgrades any existing randomized-smoothing certified-defense method from isotropic (uniform-variance) noise to anisotropic (per-dimension mean/variance) noise. Its own contributions are: (1) Theorem 2 / Corollary 3, a universal transformation that takes any existing isotropic randomized-smoothing certification (Gaussian, Laplace, exponential-inf, uniform, power-law, across l1/l2/l_inf norms) and derives the corresponding anisotropic certified radius via an invertible linear map ε' = ε^T Σ + μ; (2) a new auxiliary certification metric, the Alternative Lebesgue Measure (ALM), that captures the volume of the true (super-ellipsoidal) certified region rather than just the conservative worst-direction lp-ball radius (Theorem 6); (3) three concrete Noise Parameter Generators (NPGs) -- pattern-wise (fixed spatial variance patterns), dataset-wise (a GAN-style constant-input MLP generator trained jointly with the classifier), and certification-wise (a small CNN with 'dense blocks' that maps each individual input to its own mean/variance maps) -- offering increasing optimality at increasing computational cost; and (4) Theorem 7 with a full proof, which identifies and closes a soundness pitfall in prior input-dependent-noise smoothing methods (ANCER, RANCER) by making noise parameters depend only on the clean input x (not on x+delta), preserving the requirement that certification and perturbed-prediction share the same noise distribution.
threat_model: Standard certified-robustness threat model for randomized smoothing: a classifier f (arbitrary, deterministic or randomized) is attacked by an adversary who can apply any perturbation delta bounded in an lp-norm ball (l1, l2, or l_inf) around a clean input x; the defense must provide a provable (not merely empirical) guarantee that the smoothed classifier's prediction is unchanged for all such delta within the certified radius. The paper explicitly does not assume a specific attack algorithm -- certification is attack-agnostic by construction, in contrast to empirical defenses like adversarial training which the paper notes can be broken by adaptive/stronger adversaries. The paper also considers a subtler 'threat' internal to the defense literature itself: it argues (Section V-A) that certain prior input-dependent-noise smoothing schemes (ANCER, RANCER, cited as [20, 47]) have a soundness gap because their noise parameters vary with the (unknown, perturbed) input x+delta rather than only the clean x, which can silently invalidate the certification guarantee; UCAN's certification-wise NPG is designed to close this gap by construction (noise depends only on clean x).
limitations: The paper itself states: (1) the general (full-covariance) anisotropic theory (Theorem 2) requires an invertible d x d covariance matrix, which is computationally intractable for high-dimensional inputs (e.g., d=150,528 for ImageNet) -- the paper therefore restricts its practical method to independent (diagonal-covariance) anisotropic noise, foregoing cross-dimension correlations; (2) the certified radius (a strict lp-ball guarantee) is fundamentally controlled by min{sigma}, the smallest per-dimension scale, so radius-based certification cannot exceed what the worst dimension allows, motivating the introduction of the auxiliary (non-formal-guarantee) ALM metric which the paper is explicit is 'an auxiliary metric for the size of certified region instead of a formal guarantee'; (3) the paper reports that at some low radii on MNIST and ImageNet its certified accuracy is smaller than SOTA, attributed to lower base-classifier training performance rather than a property of the anisotropic-noise theory; (4) for the Ani. Uniform noise variant on l1 perturbations (CIFAR10), certified accuracy collapses to 0% beyond radius 2.0, an instability not shared by the Gaussian/Laplace variants; (5) evaluation on ImageNet uses only 500 randomly sampled test images rather than the full test set, following common practice in this literature but a smaller sample than MNIST/CIFAR10's full test sets. No adaptive-attack empirical robustness evaluation is presented since this is a certified (provable) defense paper, not an empirical one; the guarantees are proof-based rather than attack-tested.

quantitative_results: CIFAR10, l2, radius 2.25: UCAN certified-accuracy-w.r.t.-radius = 65%, +182.6% improvement over best baseline
CIFAR10, l2, radius 2.00: UCAN = 68%, +151.9% improvement over best baseline
MNIST, l2, radius 2.25: UCAN certified-accuracy-w.r.t.-radius = 97%, +142.5% improvement over best baseline
ImageNet, l2, largest evaluated radius: +121.1% improvement over best baseline (per Section VI-C summary)
CIFAR10, l1, radius 2.0: anisotropic Laplace = 61% certified accuracy vs. <=25% for Teng et al./Yang et al. baselines
CIFAR10, l1, radius 1.5: anisotropic Gaussian = 72% vs. 55% (Yang et al.)
CIFAR10, l_inf, 6/255: anisotropic Gaussian = 73% vs. 31-38% (Cohen et al., Zhang et al.)
MNIST, l2, radius 2.25, ALM-based improvement over best baseline: +135.0%
Dataset-wise NPG achieves up to ~6x ALM on MNIST (ALM axis reaching 6)
baselines_compared: Cohen et al. (isotropic Gaussian randomized smoothing) [12]
